And He struck a universal parable for His blessings and what the matter of disbelief in them leads to, saying: **وَضَرَبَ اللهُ مَثَلاً قَرْيَةً كانَتْ آمِنَةً مُطْمَئِنَّةً يَأْتِيها رِزْقُها رَغَداً مِنْ كُلِّ مَكانٍ فَكَفَرَتْ بِأَنْعُمِ اللهِ فَأَذاقَهَا اللهُ لِباسَ الْجُوعِ وَالْخَوْفِ بِما كانُوا يَصْنَعُونَ** — "Allah draws a parable: A town that was secure and at rest, its provision coming to it abundantly from every quarter, but it was ungrateful for Allah’s blessings. So Allah made it taste the garment of hunger and fear because of what they used to do" (16:112).

Thus, the verse—namely His saying: **الْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ** until His saying: **دِيناً**—announces that the religion of the Muslims is in safety from the side of the disbelievers, protected from the danger directed from them, and that no corruption or destruction can seep into it except from the Muslims themselves. This would only be through their disbelief in this complete blessing and their rejection of this perfect, approved religion. On that day, Allah will strip them of His blessing and change it into retribution, making them taste the garment of hunger and fear. And they did, and He did.

Whoever wishes to realize the extent of the truth of this verse in its prophecy—derived from His saying: **فَلا تَخْشَوْهُمْ وَاخْشَوْنِ**—should reflect upon the state in which the Islamic world has settled today, then trace back by analyzing historical events until he reaches the origins and roots of the issues.

The verses of mastership (الولاية) in the Qur'an have a complete connection to the warning and threat in this verse. Allah has not warned the servants about Himself in His Book except in the chapter of mastership (الولاية), saying therein time and again: **وَيُحَذِّرُكُمُ اللهُ نَفْسَهُ** — "and Allah warns you of Himself" (3:28, 30). To pursue this inquiry further would be to exit the scope of this book.

His saying, the Exalted: **الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَأَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِي وَرَضِيتُ لَكُمُ الْإِسْلامَ دِيناً** — "Today I have perfected your religion for you, and I have completed My blessing upon you, and I have approved Islam as your religion" (5:3). Perfection (الإكمال) and completion (الإتمام) are close in meaning. Al-Raghib said: **The perfection of a thing (كمال الشيء)** is the attainment of what is intended by it. And he said: **The completion of a thing (تمام الشيء)** is its reaching a limit where it does not need anything external to it. And **the deficient (الناقص)** is that which needs something external to it.

You may distinguish the meaning of the two terms in another way: the effects of things that have effects are of two types. One type is that which follows from the thing upon the existence of all its parts—if it has parts—such that if it lacks any of its parts or conditions, that effect does not follow, like fasting (الصوم); it is invalidated if one fails to abstain during part of the day. A thing being of this description is called "complete" (التمام). Allah, the Exalted, said: **ثُمَّ أَتِمُّوا الصِّيامَ إِلَى اللَّيْلِ** — "Then complete the fast until nightfall" (2:187). And He said: **وَتَمَّتْ كَلِمَةُ رَبِّكَ صِدْقاً وَعَدْلاً** — "The word of your Lord has been completed in truth and justice" (6:115).

The other type: the effect that follows from the thing without depending on the attainment of all its parts; rather, the effect of the whole is like the sum of the effects of the parts. Whenever a part exists, an effect follows from it according to its measure, and if the whole exists, the entire required effect follows from it. Allah, the Exalted, said: **فَمَنْ لَمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيامُ ثَلاثَةِ أَيَّامٍ فِي الْحَجِّ وَسَبْعَةٍ إِذا رَجَعْتُمْ تِلْكَ عَشَرَةٌ كامِلَةٌ** — "But he who cannot afford [the offering] shall fast three days during the Hajj and seven when you return; that is ten complete [days]" (2:196). And He said: **وَلِتُكْمِلُوا الْعِدَّةَ** — "and so that you may complete the number" (2:185). For this number, the effect follows from part of it just as it follows from the whole. It is said: "So-and-so's affair was completed (تم) and his intellect was perfected (كمل)"; it is not said "his intellect was completed (تم) and his affair was perfected (كمل)."

As for the difference between *ikmal* (إكمال) and *takmil* (تكميل), and likewise between *itmam* (إتمام) and *tatmim* (تتميم), it is the difference between the forms *if'al* (إفعال) and *taf'il* (تفعيل). The form *if'al*, according to its origin, indicates a single instance (الدفعة), while *taf'il* indicates gradualness (التدريج), even if linguistic expansion or evolution sometimes treats the two forms by shifting them to what is distant from the simple verb or their origin—such as *ihsan* and *tahsin*, *isdaq* and *tasdiq*, *imdad* and *tamdid*, *ifrat* and *tafrit*, and so on. These are meanings that arose according to the specificities of the contexts and then became established in the word through usage.

What follows from the above is that His saying: **أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَأَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِي** — "I have perfected your religion for you, and I have completed My blessing upon you" (5:3) implies that what is meant by "the religion" (الدين) is the sum total of the revealed knowledge and rulings, and something has been added to their number today. And "the blessing" (النعمة), whatever it may be, is a single spiritual matter that was as if deficient and without effect, then it was completed and the effect expected from it followed.

Blessing (النعمة) is a type of construction (بناء نوع) and it is that which suits the nature of a thing without its refusal. Things, although they are connected, linked, and suited to one another according to their occurrence in the system of management (نظام التدبير)—and most or all of them are blessings when added to some other assumed thing, as Allah, the Exalted, said: **وَإِنْ تَعُدُّوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ لا تُحْصُوها** — "If you enumerate Allah’s blessings, you will not be able to count them" (14:34), and He said: **وَأَسْبَغَ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعَمَهُ ظاهِرَةً وَباطِنَةً** — "and He has showered His blessings upon you, outward and inward" (31:20).

However, He, the Exalted, described some of them as evil, meanness, play, diversion, and other non-praiseworthy descriptions, as He said: **وَلا يَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَنَّما نُمْلِي لَهُمْ خَيْرٌ لِأَنْفُسِهِمْ إِنَّما نُمْلِي لَهُمْ لِيَزْدادُوا إِثْماً وَلَهُمْ عَذابٌ مُهِينٌ** — "Let the faithless not suppose that the respite We grant them is good for their souls: We only grant them respite that they may increase in sin, and there is a humiliating punishment for them" (3:178). And He said: **وَما هذِهِ الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا إِلَّا لَهْوٌ وَلَعِبٌ وَإِنَّ الدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ لَهِيَ الْحَيَوانُ** — "The life of this world is nothing but diversion and play, but the abode of the Hereafter is indeed Life" (29:64). And He said: **لا يَغُرَّنَّكَ تَقَلُّبُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فِي الْبِلادِ مَتاعٌ قَلِيلٌ ثُمَّ مَأْواهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ وَبِئْسَ الْمِهادُ** — "Never be misled by the bustle of the faithless in the towns. It is a brief enjoyment; then their abode shall be hell, and it is an evil resting place" (3:196-197), and so on.

The verses indicate that these things counted as blessings are only a blessing if they agree with the divine purpose of their creation for the sake of man. For they were only created to be a divine aid for man to utilize in the path of his true happiness, which is proximity to Him, the Glorified, through servitude (العبودية) and submission to Lordship (الربوبية). Allah, the Exalted, said: **وَما خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنْسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ** — "I did not create the jinn and the humans except that they may worship Me" (51:56).

Thus, everything man utilizes to travel toward the presence of proximity to Allah and to seek His pleasure is a blessing; if the matter is reversed, it becomes a retribution (نقمة) in his regard. Things in themselves are neutral; they are only a blessing because they contain the spirit of servitude and enter—by virtue of the aforementioned utilization—under the mastership of Allah (ولاية الله), which is the management of Lordship over the servant's affairs. The necessary consequence is that the blessing in reality is the divine mastership (الولاية الإلهية), and that a thing only becomes a blessing if it contains something of it. Allah, the Exalted, said: **اللهُ وَلِيُّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا يُخْرِجُهُمْ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ** — "Allah is the Master of the faithful: He brings them out of darkness into light" (2:257). And He, the Exalted, said: **ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللهَ مَوْلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَأَنَّ الْكافِرِينَ لا مَوْلى لَهُمْ** — "That is because Allah is the Master of the faithful, and because the faithless have no master" (47:11). And He said regarding His Messenger: **فَلا وَرَبِّكَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ حَتَّى يُحَكِّمُوكَ فِيما شَجَرَ بَيْنَهُمْ ثُمَّ لا يَجِدُوا فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ حَرَجاً مِمَّا قَضَيْتَ وَيُسَلِّمُوا تَسْلِيماً** — "But no, by your Lord! They will not believe until they make you a judge in their disputes, then do not find within their souls any burning regret over your verdict and submit in total submission" (4:65), and so on.

Thus, Islam—which is the sum total of what was revealed from Allah, the Glorified, for His servants to worship Him by—is a religion (دين). From the perspective of its containing—in terms of acting upon it—the mastership of Allah, the mastership of His Messenger, and the masters of the affair (أولياء الأمر) after him, it is a blessing (نعمة).

The mastership of Allah, the Glorified—that is, His management through the religion of His servants' affairs—is not complete except through the mastership of His Messenger, and the mastership of His Messenger is not complete except through the mastership of the masters of the affair after him. This is their management of the nation's religious affairs by permission from Allah. Allah, the Exalted, said: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ** — "O you who have faith! Obey Allah and obey the Apostle and those vested with authority among you" (4:59); the discussion on the meaning of the verse has already passed. And He said: **إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللهُ وَرَسُولُهُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا الَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ الصَّلاةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ وَهُمْ راكِعُونَ** — "Your guardian is only Allah, His Apostle, and the faithful who maintain the prayer and give the zakat while bowing down" (5:55); the discussion on the meaning of the verse will come, if Allah wills.

The sum of the verse's meaning is: Today—which is the day in which the faithless despaired of your religion—I have perfected for you the sum of the religious knowledge that I revealed to you by ordaining the mastership (الولاية). And I have completed My blessing upon you, which is the mastership—the administration and management of religious affairs in a divine manner. For until today, it was the mastership of Allah and His Messenger, which is only sufficient as long as revelation is descending; it is not sufficient for the time after that when revelation is cut off and there is no Messenger among the people to protect the religion of Allah and defend it. Rather, it is obligatory to appoint one who stands for that, and he is the master of the affair (ولي الأمر) after the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, the sustainer of the affairs of the religion and the nation.

Thus, the mastership is a single legislated entity; it was deficient and not complete until it was completed by the appointment of the master of the affair after the Prophet.

When the religion is perfected in its legislation, and the blessing of mastership is completed, then I have approved for you, in terms of religion, Islam—which is the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — in which none is worshipped except Allah, and none is obeyed—and obedience is worship—except Allah and those whose obedience He has commanded, whether Messenger or Master.

The verse thus announces that the believers today are in safety after their fear, and that Allah has approved for them to follow Islam as their religion, which is the religion of monotheism. They must worship Him and not associate anything with Him by obeying other than Allah or those whose obedience He has commanded. If you reflect upon His saying, the Exalted: **وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَيَسْتَخْلِفَنَّهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ كَمَا اسْتَخْلَفَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ وَلَيُمَكِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ دِينَهُمُ الَّذِي ارْتَضى لَهُمْ وَلَيُبَدِّلَنَّهُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ خَوْفِهِمْ أَمْناً يَعْبُدُونَنِي لا يُشْرِكُونَ بِي شَيْئاً وَمَنْ كَفَرَ بَعْدَ ذلِكَ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْفاسِقُونَ** — "Allah has promised those of you who have faith and do righteous deeds that He will surely make them successors in the earth, even as He made those who were before them successors, and He will surely establish for them their religion which He has approved for them, and that He will surely replace their fear with security: ‘They shall worship Me, not ascribing any partners to Me.’ And whoever is faithless after that—it is they who are the transgressors" (24:55), then apply the segments of this verse to the segments of His saying: **الْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ دِينِكُمْ** (etc.), you will find the verse of Surah *al-Ma’idah* to be among the instances of fulfilling the promise contained in the verse of Surah *al-Nur*, provided that His saying: **يَعْبُدُونَنِي لا يُشْرِكُونَ بِي شَيْئاً** is directed as the ultimate goal, as perhaps suggested by His saying: **وَمَنْ كَفَرَ بَعْدَ ذلِكَ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْفاسِقُونَ**.

Surah *al-Nur* was revealed before *al-Ma’idah*, as indicated by its inclusion of the story of the slander (الإفك), the verse of the *jald* (آية الجلد), the verse of the veil (آية الحجاب), and so on.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَمَنِ اضْطُرَّ فِي مَخْمَصَةٍ غَيْرَ مُتَجانِفٍ لِإِثْمٍ فَإِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ** — "But should anyone be compelled by hunger, without inclining to sin, then Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful" (5:3). *Al-makhmashah* (المخمصة) is hunger, and *al-tajanuf* (التجانف) is inclining, from *al-janaf* (الجنف) with a *jim*, which is the outward leaning of the feet, as opposed to *al-hanaf* (الحنف) with a *ha*, which is their inward leaning.

In the context of the verse, there is an indication, firstly, that the ruling is a secondary, emergency ruling (حكم ثانوي اضطراري); secondly, that the permission and lawfulness are measured by the extent to which the emergency is removed and the pain of hunger is stilled; and thirdly, that the attribute of forgiveness (المغفرة), and likewise mercy (الرحمة), just as they relate to sins necessitating punishment, can also relate to their source, which is the ruling whose violation entails the realization of the title of sin, which in turn entails punishment.

## ( A Scholarly Inquiry in Three Sections )

**1 - Beliefs Regarding the Eating of Meat:** There is no doubt that man, like other animals and plants, is equipped with a digestive system through which he attracts to himself material parts that he can process to incorporate into his body and preserve his survival. Thus, there is no natural obstacle to his eating what can be swallowed and gulped, unless he refrains from it due to harm or aversion.

As for **harm**, it is when he finds the food physically harmful to his body due to toxicity or the like, so he refrains from eating. Or he finds the eating spiritually harmful, such as the prohibitions in various religions and laws; this type is an intellectual refrainment from eating.

As for **aversion** (التنفر), it is the feeling of filthiness (الاستقذار) with which the nature (الطبع) refrains from approaching it, just as man does not eat his own excrement due to his finding it filthy. This has been observed in some children and the insane. To this is added what is based on belief-based factors such as religion or various traditions prevalent in diverse societies. For example, Muslims find the flesh of swine filthy, while Christians find it palatable. Westerners feed on many types of animals that Easterners find filthy, such as crabs, frogs, mice, and others. This type of refrainment is by the second nature (الطبع الثاني) and the acquired disposition.

It has thus become clear that man follows different paths in feeding on meat, ranging from absolute unrestraint to total refrainment, and that his deeming lawful what he deems lawful is a following of nature, just as his refraining from what he refrains from is due to intellect or a secondary nature.

The tradition of Buddha prohibited the eating of animal flesh entirely; this is an extreme of deficiency (تفريط) countered on the side of excess (إفراط) by what was common among primitive peoples in Africa and elsewhere, who ate all types of meat, even human flesh.

The Arabs used to eat the meat of livestock (الأنعام) and other animals, even things like mice and lizards. They ate from the livestock what they killed by slaughter and the like, and they ate other things such as carrion (الميتة) in all its forms, such as the strangled (المنخنقة), the beaten to death (الموقوذة), the fallen (المتردية), the gored (النطيحة), and what the predator had eaten from. One of them would say: "What is the matter with you that you eat what you have killed but do not eat what Allah has killed?!"—as many might utter today. One of them says: "What is the difference between meat and meat if it does not harm the human body, even through technical medical treatment?" The digestive system does not distinguish between this and that.

The Arabs also used to eat blood; they would fill intestines with blood, roast them, and feed them to guests. When they faced drought, they would wound their camels with arrowheads and drink the blood that came out. The eating of blood is common today among many non-Muslim nations.

The people of China from the idolaters have an even broader tradition; they—according to what is reported—eat all kinds of animals, even dogs and cats, and even worms, shells, and other insects.

Islam has taken a middle path in this, allowing meat that moderate human natures can tolerate. Then it specified among the four-legged animals the livestock (البهائم) such as sheep, goats, cattle, and camels, with a dislike (كراهية) for some such as horses and donkeys. Among birds—excluding birds of prey—those that have a crop (حوصلة), flap their wings (دفيف), and have no talons (مخلب). And among sea animals, some types of fish, according to the details mentioned in the books of jurisprudence.

Then it prohibited their blood, every carrion from them, and what was not purified by dedicating it (الإهلال) to Allah, exalted is His Name. The purpose in this is to revive the tradition of the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — which is man's inclination toward the principle of eating meat, while respecting sound thought and the upright nature that refrain from allowing what is generally harmful, and allowing what is found filthy and aversive.

**2 - How was the killing of animals commanded when mercy forbids it?** A questioner might ask: "The animal is a being with a conscious soul that feels the pain of torment and the bitterness of annihilation and death just as man does. The instinct of self-love that drives us to be wary of every disliked thing and to flee from the pain of torment and death demands mercy for others of the species, because what pains us pains them, and what is difficult for us is difficult for them; souls are equal."

"This analogy applies exactly to other types of animals. How then is it permissible for us to torment them with what we are tormented by, to exchange for them the sweetness of life for the bitterness of death, and to deprive them of the blessing of survival, which is the noblest blessing? Allah, the Glorified, is the Most Merciful of the merciful; how can His mercy encompass commanding the killing of an animal for a human to enjoy, when they are both equal in being His creation?"

The answer to this is that it is the imposition of emotions over realities, and legislation only follows true interests (المصالح الحقيقية), not illusory emotions.

To clarify this: if you trace the existents under your observation with what is available to you, you will find them in their formation and survival following the law of transformation (ناموس التحول). There is nothing except that it is possible for it to transform into another, and for the other to transform into it, without a medium or through a medium. One does not exist except that another is negated, and this does not remain except that that one perishes. The world of matter is the world of exchange and transformation; or if you wish, say: the world of the eater and the eaten.

Thus, terrestrial compounds "eat" the earth by annexing it to themselves and shaping it into a form that suits them or is specific to them; then the earth "eats" them and annihilates them.

Then the plant feeds on the earth and inhales the air; then the earth eats it and decomposes it into its original parts and primary elements, and one of them continues to return to the other.

Then the animal feeds on the plant and water and inhales the air. Some of its types feed on others, such as predators eating the meat of others by hunting. Birds of prey eat things like pigeons and sparrows; they cannot, according to the digestive system specific to them, do otherwise. These feed on grains and things like flies, gnats, and mosquitoes, which in turn feed on the blood of humans and other animals, and so on. Then the earth eats everyone.

So the system of creation (نظام التكوين) and the law of nature (ناموس الخلقة) which has absolute, followed authority over existents is what established the ruling of feeding on meat and the like, then guided the parts of existence to that. It is what fashioned man in a fashion suitable for feeding on animals and plants alike. In the front of his digestive apparatus are his teeth, arranged in an arrangement suitable for cutting, breaking, tearing, and grinding—incisors, canines, and molars. He is not like the sheep and cattle among livestock that cannot tear and rend, nor is he like the predators that cannot grind and chew.

Then the sense of taste prepared in his mouth that finds the taste of meat delicious, then the desire deposited in the rest of his digestive organs—all these find meat palatable and crave it. All of that is a creative guidance (هداية تكوينية) and a permission from the Trustee of Creation. Is it possible to distinguish between creative guidance and the permission for the act guided toward, by accepting one and denying the other?

Islam is a primordial religion (دين فطري) whose only concern is to revive the traces of the primordial nature (الفطرة) that human ignorance has effaced. Thus, there is no escape from allowing what creation guides toward and nature decrees.

Just as it revives this natural ruling through legislation, it revives other rulings established by the Establisher of Creation, which is what was previously mentioned regarding the obstacles to unrestraint in the ruling of feeding—namely, the intellect's ruling on the obligation of avoiding what contains physical or spiritual harm among meats, and the ruling of internal feelings and emotions to be wary of and refrain from what upright natures find filthy and aversive. These two rulings also have their origins in an act of creation. Islam has considered them, prohibiting what harms the growth of the body, and prohibiting what harms the interests of human society—such as what is dedicated to other than Allah, what is gained through gambling (الميسر) and seeking omens with arrows (الاستقسام بالأزلام), and the like—and it prohibited the foul things (الخبائث) that natures find filthy.

As for the talk of mercy (الرحمة) preventing torment and killing, there is no doubt that mercy is a subtle creative gift deposited in the nature of man and many of the animals we have considered. However, creation did not bring it into existence to govern affairs with absolute authority and be obeyed with absolute obedience. Creation itself does not employ mercy in an absolute manner; were that the case, no trace of pain, disease, calamity, or types of torment would exist in the realm of existence.

Furthermore, human mercy in itself is not an absolutely virtuous character like justice. Were it so, it would not be good to hold a wrongdoer accountable for his wrongdoing, nor to recompense a criminal for his crime, nor to meet aggression with aggression, in which lies the destruction of the earth and those upon it.

Despite this, Islam did not neglect the matter of mercy insofar as it is one of the gifts of creation. It commanded the spreading of mercy generally, forbade the agonizing of the animal during killing, forbade the cutting of the limbs of the slaughtered animal or skinning it before its soul has departed—and from this chapter is the prohibition of the strangled and the beaten to death—and forbade the killing of an animal while another is looking at it. It established for purification (التذكية) the rulings most gentle to the slaughtered animal, commanded offering water to it, and so on, the details of which are found in the books of jurisprudence.

With all that, Islam is a religion of intellect (التعقل), not a religion of emotion. It does not prioritize the ruling of emotion over the rulings that benefit the system of human society, nor does it consider from it except what the intellect considers. The reference for that is following the ruling of the intellect.

As for the talk of divine mercy and that He, the Exalted, is the Most Merciful of the merciful, He, the Exalted, is not described with mercy in the sense of tenderness of heart or a specific emotional affect that drives the merciful to be kind to the one shown mercy. For that is a physical, material attribute; He is far exalted above that. Rather, its meaning is His, the Exalted, bestowing of good upon the one deserving it according to the measure of his desert. For this reason, what we count as torment may be a mercy from Him, the Exalted, and vice versa. It is not permissible in wisdom to nullify one of the interests of management in legislation in following what is suggested by the false emotion of mercy within us, or to be lax in making the laws correspond to realities.

It has thus become clear from all that has passed that Islam, in allowing the eating of meat and in the restrictions with which it restricted the permission and the conditions it stipulated, all mimic the matter of nature: the nature of Allah upon which He fashioned people; there is no change to the creation of Allah; that is the upright religion!

**3 - Why was Islam built upon purification (التذكية)?** This is another question branching from the previous one: "We have accepted that the eating of meat is among the things allowed by nature and creation. Why then was it not restricted to what is obtained by chance and the like, by restricting meat to what is provided by natural death (حتف الأنف)? Thus, one would combine the ruling of creation on permission with the ruling of mercy on refraining from agonizing the animal and distressing it by killing or slaughter, without deviating from that to purification (التذكية) and slaughter?"

The answer to this has become clear from what preceded in the second section. Mercy in this sense is not obligatory to follow; rather, following it leads to the nullification of the rulings of realities. You have known that Islam, despite this, spared no effort in commanding the exercise of mercy as much as possible in this chapter, to preserve this subtle trait among the species.

Furthermore, restricting permission to carrion (الميتة) and its like—which results in nothing but the corruption of the temperament and harm to bodies—is itself contrary to mercy. Beyond all that, it is not devoid of general hardship (الحرج العام) which must be negated.

## ( Discussion on Narrations )

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, narrated from Ikrimah from Ibn Abbas, who said: "No verse was revealed: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** (O you who have faith) except that Ali is its noble and its commander. Allah has indeed censured the companions of Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله in more than one place—but He never mentioned Ali except with good."

I say: It is narrated in *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from Muwaffaq ibn Ahmad, from Ikrimah, from Ibn Abbas: the like of it until his saying: "and its commander." It was also narrated by al-Ayyashi from Ikrimah.

We have previously cited the hadith from *al-Durr al-Manthur*.

In some narrations from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him), he said: "There is no **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** in the Qur'an except in our right," and this is from the application (الجري) or from the inner meaning of the revelation (باطن التنزيل).

In the same source, from Abdullah ibn Sinan, who said: "I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَوْفُوا بِالْعُقُودِ** (O you who have faith! Keep your agreements). He said: 'The covenants (العهود).'"

I say: Al-Qummi also narrated it in his *tafsir* from him.

In *al-Tahdhib*, with a chain of transmission from Muhammad ibn Muslim, who said: "I asked one of the two (peace be upon them) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **أُحِلَّتْ لَكُمْ بَهِيمَةُ الْأَنْعامِ** (Livestock are made lawful for you). He said: 'The fetus in its mother's womb; if it has grown hair and wool, its purification (ذكاة) is the purification of its mother, which is what Allah, the Exalted, intended.'"

I say: The hadith is narrated in *al-Kafi* and *al-Faqih* from him from one of the two. Al-Ayyashi narrated this meaning in his *tafsir* from Muhammad ibn Muslim from one of the two, and from Zurarah from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him). Al-Qummi narrated it in his *tafsir*, and it was narrated in *al-Majma'* from Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah (peace be upon them).

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تُحِلُّوا شَعائِرَ اللهِ** (O you who have faith! Do not violate the sacraments of Allah) (the verse). The sacraments (الشعائر): the *ihram*, the *tawaf*, the prayer at the Station of Ibrahim, the *sa'y* between Safa and Marwah, and all the rituals (المناسك) are among the sacraments of Allah. Among the sacraments is when a man drives a sacrificial camel (بدنة) during Hajj—then marks it (أشعرها), i.e., cuts its hump or skin—or collars it (قلدها) so people know it is an offering (هدي)—then no one should interfere with it. They were only called sacraments (شعائر) so that people would be notified (يشعر) of them and recognize them. His saying: **وَلَا الشَّهْرَ الْحَرامَ** (nor the sacred month)—it is *Dhu al-Hijjah*, and it is among the sacred months. His saying: **وَلَا الْهَدْيَ** (nor the offering)—it is what the person in *ihram* drives when he enters *ihram*. His saying: **وَلَا الْقَلائِدَ** (nor the garlands)—he said: he collars it with the sandal in which he has prayed. His saying: **وَلَا آمِّينَ الْبَيْتَ الْحَرامَ** (nor those making for the Sacred House)—he said: those who perform Hajj to the House.

In *al-Majma'*, Abu Ja'far al-Baqir (peace be upon him) said: "This verse was revealed regarding a man from the Banu Rabi'ah called al-Hatam."

He said: And al-Suddi said: "Al-Hatam ibn Hind al-Bakri approached until he came to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله alone—leaving his horses outside Medina. He said: 'To what do you call?' The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله had said to his companions: 'A man from the Banu Rabi'ah will enter upon you today—speaking with the tongue of a devil.' When the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله answered him, he said: 'Give me time, perhaps I will submit, and I have those I must consult.' So he left him, and the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'He entered with the face of a disbeliever and left with the heels of a traitor.' He passed by one of the pastures of Medina and drove it away—departing with it while reciting poetry:

*He has wrapped it in the night with a crushing driver*
*Neither a shepherd of camels nor of sheep*
*Nor a butcher on the back of a chopping block*
*They spent the night sleeping while the son of Hind did not sleep*
*A youth like an arrow spent the night enduring it*
*With plump legs and smooth feet*

Then he approached the following year as a pilgrim having collared an offering—so the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله wanted to send someone to him, and this verse was revealed: **وَلَا آمِّينَ الْبَيْتَ الْحَرامَ** (nor those making for the Sacred House)."

He said: And Ibn Zayd said: "It was revealed on the Day of the Conquest—regarding people from the polytheists making for the House, chanting the *talbiyah* for an *umrah*. The Muslims said: 'O Messenger of Allah—these are polytheists like those; let us raid them.' So Allah, the Exalted, revealed the verse."

I say: Al-Tabari narrated the story from al-Suddi and Ikrimah, and the second story from Ibn Zayd. It is narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur*, the second story from Ibn Abi Hatim from Zayd ibn Aslam, and in it: that it was on the Day of *al-Hudaybiyyah*. Both stories do not agree with what is almost universally accepted among the commentators and transmitters that Surah *al-Ma’idah* was revealed during the Farewell Pilgrimage. For if that were the case, His saying: **إِنَّمَا الْمُشْرِكُونَ نَجَسٌ فَلا يَقْرَبُوا الْمَسْجِدَ الْحَرامَ بَعْدَ عامِهِمْ هذا** — "Indeed the polytheists are unclean; so let them not approach the Holy Mosque after this year of theirs" (9:28), and His saying: **فَاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدْتُمُوهُمْ** — "then kill the polytheists wherever you find them" (9:5)—both verses would have been revealed before His saying: **وَلَا آمِّينَ الْبَيْتَ الْحَرامَ**, and there would be no place then for the prohibition of interfering with the polytheists if they intended the Sacred House.

Perhaps one of these two stories or something similar is the reason for what was narrated from Ibn Abbas, Mujahid, Qatadah, and al-Dahhak: that His saying: **وَلَا آمِّينَ الْبَيْتَ الْحَرامَ** is abrogated (منسوخ) by His saying: **فَاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدْتُمُوهُمْ** (the verse) and His saying: **إِنَّمَا الْمُشْرِكُونَ نَجَسٌ فَلا يَقْرَبُوا الْمَسْجِدَ الْحَرامَ** (the verse). The mention of abrogation (النسخ) occurred in *Tafsir al-Qummi*, and its appearance is that it is a narration.

Despite all that, the late revelation of Surah *al-Ma’idah* refutes all of this. It has been narrated through the paths of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) that it is abrogating (ناسخة) and not abrogated (منسوخة). Furthermore, His saying, the Exalted, therein: **الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ** (the verse) refuses that abrogation should occur to any of its verses. Based on this, the import of His saying: **وَلَا آمِّينَ الْبَيْتَ الْحَرامَ** is as if explained by His saying afterward: **وَلا يَجْرِمَنَّكُمْ شَنَآنُ قَوْمٍ أَنْ صَدُّوكُمْ عَنِ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ أَنْ تَعْتَدُوا** — "and ill-feeling for a people because they barred you from the Holy Mosque should not make you transgress" (5:2), meaning: do not violate the sanctity of the House by interfering with those making for it because of an interference from them against you before this, nor others among those who barred you previously from the Sacred House, such that you transgress against them with sin like killing, or aggression like what is less than killing of injustice; rather, cooperate in righteousness and godwariness.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ahmad and ‘Abd ibn Humayd narrated regarding this verse—meaning His saying: **وَتَعاوَنُوا عَلَى الْبِرِّ** (And cooperate in piety) [al-Ma'idah: 2]—and al-Bukhari in his *Tarikh* narrated from Wabishah, who said: "I came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) intending not to leave any matter of piety (البر) or sin (الإثم) without asking him about it. He said to me: 'O Wabishah, shall I inform you of what you came to ask about, or will you ask?' I said: 'O Messenger of Allah, inform me.' He said: 'You came to ask about piety and sin.' Then he gathered his three fingers and began tapping my chest with them, saying: 'O Wabishah, seek a verdict (استفت) from your heart; seek a verdict from your soul. Piety (البر) is that with which the heart finds rest and the soul finds tranquility, and sin (الإثم) is that which wavers in the heart and hesitates in the chest, even if people give you a verdict [to the contrary] and continue to do so.'"

In the same source: Ahmad, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, ibn Hibban, al-Tabarani, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), and al-Bayhaqi narrated from Abu Umamah: "A man asked the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) about sin (الإثم), and he said: 'Whatever wavers in your soul, leave it.'"

He asked: "Then what is faith (الإيمان)?" He replied: "Whoever is grieved by his bad deed and gladdened by his good deed is a believer (مؤمن)."

In the same source: Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ahmad, al-Bukhari in *al-Adab*, Muslim, al-Tirmidhi, al-Hakim, and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Shu‘ab* narrated from al-Nawwas ibn Sam‘an, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was asked about piety and sin, and he said: 'Piety (البر) is good character, and sin (الإثم) is what wavers in your soul and you would dislike people to find out about.'"

I say: These narrations—as you can see—are built upon His saying, the Exalted: **وَنَفْسٍ وَما سَوَّاها فَأَلْهَمَها فُجُورَها وَتَقْواها** (By the soul and Him who fashioned it, and inspired it with [the discernment of] its wickedness and its godwariness) (al-Shams: 7-8), and they support what was previously mentioned regarding the meaning of sin (الإثم).

In *al-Majma‘*: There is a difference of opinion regarding this—meaning His saying: **وَلَا آمِّينَ الْبَيْتَ الْحَرامَ** (nor those making for the Sacred House) [al-Ma'idah: 2]. It was said: "It is abrogated (منسوخ) by His saying: **فَاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدْتُمُوهُمْ** (then slay the polytheists wherever you find them) [al-Tawbah: 5]"—this is from most of the commentators. And it was said: "Nothing from this Surah was abrogated, nor from this verse, because it is not permissible to initiate fighting against the polytheists in the sacred months unless they initiate the fight." Then he [al-Tabarsi] said: "And this is what is narrated from Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام)."

In *al-Faqih*, with its chain of transmission to Aban ibn Taghlib from Abu Ja‘far Muhammad ibn ‘Ali al-Baqir (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), he said: "Carrion (الميتة), blood (الدم), and the flesh of swine (لحم الخنزير) are well-known. 'That which has been offered to other than Allah' (ما أهل لغير الله به) means what was slaughtered for idols. As for 'the strangled' (المنخنقة), the Magians used not to eat slaughtered animals but ate carrion; they would strangle cows and sheep, and when they were strangled and died, they would eat them. 'The beaten to death' (الموقوذة) was when they would tie its legs and beat it until it died, then eat it. 'The fallen to death' (المتردية) was when they would blindfold it and throw it from a roof, and when it died, they would eat it. 'The gored to death' (النطيحة) was when they would make rams gore each other, and when one died, they would eat it. 'And what wild beasts have eaten, except what you have sacrificed' (وما أكل السبع إلا ما ذكيتم)—they used to eat what the wolf, lion, or bear killed, so Allah (عز وجل) forbade that. 'And what has been slaughtered on the altars' (وما ذبح على النصب)—they used to slaughter for the fire-temples, and the Quraysh used to worship trees and rocks and slaughter for them. 'And that you should divide by raffling with arrows; that is transgression' (وأن تستقسموا بالأزلام ذلكم فسق)—he said: They would take a slaughtered camel and divide it into ten parts, then gather over it and bring out arrows, handing them to a man. The arrows were ten: seven had shares (أنصباء) and three had no shares.

Those that had shares were: *al-Fadhdh*, *al-Taw’am*, *al-Musbil*, *al-Nafis*, *al-Hils*, *al-Raqib*, and *al-Mu‘alla*. *Al-Fadhdh* had one share, *al-Taw’am* had two, *al-Musbil* had three, *al-Nafis* had four, *al-Hils* had five, *al-Raqib* had six, and *al-Mu‘alla* had seven.

Those that had no shares were: *al-Safih*, *al-Manih*, and *al-Waghd*. The price of the camel was borne by those for whom no shares came out—and this is gambling (القمار), so Allah forbade it."

I say: What is mentioned in the narration regarding the explanation of the strangled, the beaten, and the fallen is by way of illustration through examples, as appears from the following narration. Likewise, the mention of His saying: **إِلَّا ما ذَكَّيْتُمْ** (except what you have sacrificed) with His saying: **وَما أَكَلَ السَّبُعُ** (and what wild beasts have eaten), and His saying: **ذلِكُمْ فِسْقٌ** (that is transgression) with His saying: **وَأَنْ تَسْتَقْسِمُوا بِالْأَزْلامِ** (and that you should divide by raffling with arrows), carries no indication of restriction (التقييد).

In *Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi*, from ‘Ayyuq ibn Qasut, from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) regarding the saying of Allah: **الْمُنْخَنِقَةُ** (the strangled)—he said: "That which is strangled in its tether." **وَالْمَوْقُوذَةُ** (the beaten to death)—"The sick animal that does not feel the pain of slaughter, does not struggle, and no blood comes out of it." **الْمُتَرَدِّيَةُ** (the fallen to death)—"That which falls from atop a house or the like." **النَّطِيحَةُ** (the gored to death)—"That which is gored by its companion."

In the same source, from al-Hasan ibn ‘Ali al-Washsha’ from Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (عليه السلام), he said: "I heard him saying: 'The fallen, the gored, and what wild beasts have eaten—if you manage to sacrifice (ذكاته) it, then eat it.'"

In the same source, from Muhammad ibn ‘Abdillah from some of his companions, he said: "I said to Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام): 'May I be your ransom, why did Allah forbid carrion, blood, and the flesh of swine?' He said: 'Allah (تبارك وتعالى) did not forbid that to His servants—while making other things lawful for them—out of a desire (رغبة) for what He forbade them, nor out of a lack of interest (زهد) in what He made lawful for them. Rather, He created the creation and knew what sustains their bodies and what is good for them, so He made it lawful and permissible as a grace (تفضلا) from Him for their benefit. And He knew what harms them, so He prohibited them from it and forbade it to them. Then He made it permissible for the one in necessity (المضطر) and made it lawful for them at the time when his body cannot be sustained without it, so He commanded him to take from it only to the extent of sufficiency (البلغة) and nothing more.'

Then he said: 'As for carrion (الميتة), no one draws near it or eats it but that his body becomes weak, his frame wastes away, his strength fails, and his progeny is cut off; and the eater of carrion dies only suddenly.

As for blood (الدم), it bequeaths rabies (الكلب), hardness of heart (قسوة القلب), and a lack of kindness and mercy; one cannot be sure he will not kill his child or parents, nor can his close friend or companion be safe from him.

As for the flesh of swine (لحم الخنزير), Allah transformed (مسخ) a people into various forms resembling the pig, the monkey, and the bear—and whatever was of the transformed (الأمساخ)—then He forbade eating its like so that people would not benefit from them nor belittle His punishment.

As for wine (الخمر), He forbade it for its effect and corruption, and said: "The one addicted to wine is like a worshiper of an idol; it bequeaths tremors, takes away his light, destroys his manliness (مروته), and drives him to commit forbidden acts such as shedding blood and committing adultery. One cannot be sure that when he is drunk, he will respect any sanctity while he does not comprehend it. Wine leads its drinker to nothing but every evil."'"

## (Another Narrative Discussion)

In *Ghayat al-Maram*, from Abu al-Mu’ayyad Muwaffaq ibn Ahmad in the book *Fada’il ‘Ali*, he said: "The Master of Hadith-Preservers (سيد الحفاظ) Shahrdar ibn Shirawayh ibn Shahrdar al-Daylami informed me in what he wrote to me from Hamadan: Abu al-Fath ‘Abdus ibn ‘Abdillah ibn ‘Abdus al-Hamadani informed us in writing: ‘Abdullah ibn Ishaq al-Baghawi narrated to us: al-Husayn ibn ‘Alil al-Ghanawi narrated to us: Muhammad ibn ‘Abd al-Rahman al-Zarra‘ narrated to us: Qays ibn Hafs narrated to us: ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn narrated to us: Abu Hurayrah narrated to us from Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri: 'When the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) called the people to Ghadir Khumm, he ordered the thorns under the tree to be swept away—that was on Thursday. He then called the people to ‘Ali, took him by his arm, and raised it until the people saw the whiteness of his armpits. They did not part until this verse was revealed: **الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَأَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِي وَرَضِيتُ لَكُمُ الْإِسْلامَ دِيناً** (Today I have perfected your religion for you, and I have completed My blessing upon you, and I have approved Islam as your religion) [al-Ma'idah: 3]. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) then said: "Allah is Greatest (الله أكبر) for the perfection of the religion, the completion of the blessing, and the Lord’s pleasure with my message and the mastership (الولاية) of ‘Ali." Then he said: "O Allah, befriend him who befriends him, and be the enemy of him who is his enemy; help him who helps him, and forsake him who forsakes him."'"

Hassan ibn Thabit said: "Do you permit me, O Messenger of Allah, to say some verses?" He said: "Speak, for Allah (تعالى) sends it down." So Hassan ibn Thabit said:

*Their Prophet calls them on the day of Ghadir*
*At Khumm—and listen to the Prophet as a caller!*
*Saying: "Am I not your master (مولاكم)?" They said: "Yes, and our guardian (وليكم)!"*
*And they did not show any blindness there.*
*“Your God is our Master, and you are our Guardian,*
*And you shall not find among the creation any disobedient to this command.”*
*So he said to him: "Rise, O ‘Ali, for I*
*Have approved you after me as an Imam and a guide."*

And from the book *Nuzul al-Qur’an* regarding the Commander of the Faithful ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib by the Hadith-Preserver Abu Nu‘aym, tracing it back to Qays ibn al-Rabi‘, from Abu Harun al-‘Abdi, from Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri: the same was narrated, and at the end of the verses he said:

*So whomever I was his master, this is his guardian,*
*So be to him sincere helpers and allies.*
*There he prayed: "O Allah, befriend his guardian,*
*And be the enemy of him who treats ‘Ali as an enemy."*

And from *Nuzul al-Qur’an* also, tracing it back to ‘Ali ibn ‘Amir from Abu al-Hijaf from al-A‘mash from ‘Adah, who said: "This verse was revealed to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib: **يا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ** (O Apostle! Deliver what has been sent down to you) [al-Ma'idah: 67]—and Allah (تعالى) had said: **الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَأَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ** (Today I have perfected your religion for you, and I have completed...)."

And from Ibrahim ibn Muhammad al-Hamwayni, who said: "The Shaykh Taj al-Din Abu Talib ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn ibn ‘Uthman ibn ‘Abdillah al-Khazin informed me: Imam Burhan al-Din Nasir ibn Abi al-Makarim al-Mutarrizi informed me by authorization (إجازة): the Imam, the Orator of Khwarizm, Abu al-Mu’ayyad Muwaffaq ibn Ahmad al-Makki al-Khwarizmi informed me: the Master of Hadith-Preservers informed me in what he wrote to me from Hamadan: the Chief Abu al-Fath informed us in writing: ‘Abdullah ibn Ishaq al-Baghawi narrated to us: al-Hasan ibn ‘Aqil al-Ghanawi narrated to us: Muhammad ibn ‘Abdillah al-Zarra‘ narrated to us: Qays ibn Hafs narrated to us: ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn al-‘Abdi narrated to me from Abu Harun al-‘Abdi from Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri," and he mentioned the same as the first hadith.

And from al-Hamwayni also, from the Master of Hadith-Preservers and Abu Mansur Shahrdar ibn Shirawayh ibn Shahrdar al-Daylami, who said: "Al-Hasan ibn Ahmad ibn al-Hasan al-Haddad al-Muqri’ al-Hafiz informed us from Ahmad ibn ‘Abdillah ibn Ahmad: Muhammad ibn Ahmad ibn ‘Ali narrated to us: Muhammad ibn ‘Uthman ibn Abi Shaybah narrated to us: Yahya al-Himmani narrated to us: Qays ibn al-Rabi‘ narrated to us from Abu Harun al-‘Abdi from Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri," and he mentioned the same as the first hadith.

He said: Al-Hamwayni said following this hadith: "This hadith has many paths (طرق) to Abu Sa‘id Sa‘d ibn Malik al-Khudri al-Ansari."

And from *al-Manaqib al-Fakhirah* by al-Sayyid al-Radi (رحمه الله), from Muhammad ibn Ishaq, from Abu Ja‘far, from his father, from his grandfather, who said: "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) returned from the Farewell Pilgrimage, he stopped at a land called Dawjan, and this verse was revealed: **يا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ وَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلْ فَما بَلَّغْتَ رِسالَتَهُ وَاللهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ النَّاسِ** (O Apostle! Deliver what has been sent down to you from your Lord; and if you do not, you will not have delivered His message. In Allah shall protect you from the people) [al-Ma'idah: 67]. When his protection from the people was revealed, he called out: 'The prayer is congregational!' (الصلاة جامعة). The people gathered to him, and he said: 'Who is more entitled to you than your own selves?' They all cried out: 'Allah and His Messenger!' So he took the hand of ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib and said: 'Whomever I was his master, ‘Ali is his master. O Allah, befriend him who befriends him, and be the enemy of him who is his enemy; help him who helps him, and forsake him who forsakes him—for he is from me and I am from him, and he is to me as Aaron was to Moses, except that there is no prophet after me.' This was the last obligation (فريضة) that Allah (تعالى) ordained for the Ummah of Muhammad. Then Allah (تعالى) revealed to His Prophet: **الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَأَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِي وَرَضِيتُ لَكُمُ الْإِسْلامَ دِيناً** (Today I have perfected your religion for you, and I have completed My blessing upon you, and I have approved Islam as your religion)."

Abu Ja‘far said: "So they accepted from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) all that Allah had commanded them of the obligations regarding prayer, fasting, zakat, and pilgrimage, and they believed him in that."

Ibn Ishaq said: "I said to Abu Ja‘far: 'When was that?' He said: 'On the eighteenth night of Dhu al-Hijjah in the year ten, upon his return from the Farewell Pilgrimage. Between that and [the death of] the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) were one hundred days.' And twelve [men] heard the Messenger of Allah at Ghadir Khumm."

And from the *Manaqib* of ibn al-Maghazili, tracing it back to Abu Hurayrah, who said: "Whoever fasts on the eighteenth day of Dhu al-Hijjah, Allah records for him the fasting of sixty months. It is the day of Ghadir Khumm, on which the Prophet took the pledge of allegiance (بيعة) for ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib and said: 'Whomever I was his master, ‘Ali is his master. O Allah, befriend him who befriends him and be the enemy of him who is his enemy, and help him who helps him.' ‘Umar ibn al-Khattab said to him: 'Bravo, bravo (بخ بخ) to you, O son of Abi Talib! You have become my master and the master of every believing man and woman.' Then Allah (تعالى) revealed: **الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَأَتْمَمْتُ** (Today I have perfected your religion for you, and I have completed...)."

And from the *Manaqib* of ibn Mardawayh and the book *Sarqat al-Shi‘r* by al-Marzubani, from Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri, similar to what was previously mentioned from al-Khatib.

I say: Both hadiths were narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Abu Sa‘id and Abu Hurayrah, and he described their chains of transmission as weak. However, it has been narrated through many paths which—if scrutinized—reach among the companions ‘Umar ibn al-Khattab, ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib, Mu‘awiyah, and Samurah: that the verse was revealed on the Day of ‘Arafah during the Farewell Pilgrimage, which was a Friday. The most relied upon among them is what is narrated from ‘Umar; it was narrated from al-Humaydi, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, Ahmad, al-Bukhari, Muslim, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa’i, ibn Jarir, ibn al-Mundhir, ibn Hibban, and al-Bayhaqi in his *Sunan* from Tariq ibn Shihab from ‘Umar. And from ibn Rahawayh in his *Musnad* and ‘Abd ibn Humayd from Abu al-‘Aliyah from ‘Umar. And from ibn Jarir from Qabisah ibn Abi Dhu’ayb from ‘Umar. And from al-Bazzaz from ibn ‘Abbas, and it appears he narrates it from ‘Umar.

Furthermore, I say: As for his mention of the weakness of the two chains of transmission for the two hadiths, it does not benefit him in weakening the content (المتن) at all. We have clarified in the preceding commentary (البيان) that the meaning of the noble verse does not suit any of the other possibilities and meanings mentioned therein. Thus, these two narrations and what is in their meaning are the ones that agree with the Book among all the narrations, and they are the ones designated to be taken.

Moreover, these hadiths indicating the revelation of the verse regarding the matter of mastership (الولاية)—which exceed twenty hadiths from the paths of both the Sunnis and the Shi‘a—are linked to what was reported regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) of His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ** (O Apostle! Deliver what has been sent down to you from your Lord) (al-Ma'idah: 67), which exceed fifteen hadiths narrated by both groups. All of them are linked to the Hadith of Ghadir: "Whomever I was his master, ‘Ali is his master," which is a widely-transmitted (متواتر) hadith narrated from a vast number of companions, and its status as *mutawatir* has been acknowledged by many scholars of both groups.

It is agreed upon that this occurred during the return of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) from Mecca to Medina.

This mastership (الولاية)—if not taken as a joke or mockery—is an obligation (فريضة) among the obligations, like befriending (التولي) and disassociating (التبري) which the Qur’an has explicitly stated in many verses. If that is the case, it is not permissible for its establishment to be delayed after the revelation of the verse—meaning His saying: **الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ** (Today I have perfected). The verse was only revealed after its obligation was ordained by Allah, the Glorified, and no reliance is placed on narrations that contradict this, if they indeed contradict it.

As for the narrations he reported, you have seen what ought to be said regarding them. However, there is a matter that must be noted: reflecting upon the two noble verses: **يا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ وَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلْ فَما بَلَّغْتَ رِسالَتَهُ** (O Apostle! Deliver what has been sent down to you from your Lord; and if you do not, you will not have delivered His message) according to the explanation of its meaning that will follow, and His saying: **الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ** (Today I have perfected your religion for you), along with the hadiths reported from the paths of both groups regarding them and the *mutawatir* Ghadir narrations, as well as studying the internal conditions of the Islamic society at the end of the era of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and deep research into them, yields the certainty that the matter of mastership (الولاية) had been revealed days before the Day of Ghadir. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was wary of the people in manifesting it, fearing they would not receive it with acceptance or would harbor ill intentions toward it, thereby disrupting the matter of the mission (الدعوة). Thus, he continued to delay its delivery to the people from one day to the next until His saying was revealed: **يا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ** (O Apostle! Deliver), after which he did not delay it.

Based on this, it is possible that Allah, the Glorified, revealed the bulk of the Surah, including His saying: **الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ** (Today I have perfected your religion for you), and revealed with it the command of mastership, all on the Day of ‘Arafah. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) then delayed the explanation of mastership until Ghadir Khumm, having already recited its verse on the Day of ‘Arafah. As for some narrations containing its revelation on the Day of Ghadir, it is not far-fetched that this was due to his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recitation of the verse in conjunction with delivering the command of mastership, as it was regarding its status.

Accordingly, there is no contradiction between the narrations—meaning those indicating the revelation of the verse regarding the matter of mastership, and those indicating its revelation on the Day of ‘Arafah as narrated from ‘Umar, ‘Ali, Mu‘awiyah, and Samurah. Contradiction would only be established if one of the two categories indicated revelation on the Day of Ghadir Khumm and the other indicated revelation on the Day of ‘Arafah.

As for what is in the second category of narrations—that the verse indicates the perfection of the religion through the pilgrimage and the like—this is from the understanding of the narrator; the Book does not correspond to it, nor is there any explanation from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that can be relied upon.

Perhaps what we have mentioned can be inferred from what al-‘Ayyashi narrated in his *Tafsir* from Ja‘far ibn Muhammad ibn Muhammad al-Khuza‘i from his father, who said: "I heard Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) saying: 'When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) stopped at ‘Arafat on Friday, Gabriel came to him and said: "Allah conveys peace to you and says to you: Say to your Ummah: Today I have perfected your religion for you through the mastership (الولاية) of ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib, and I have completed My blessing upon you, and I have approved Islam as your religion. I shall not reveal anything to you after this; I have revealed to you prayer, zakat, fasting, and pilgrimage, and this is the fifth. I shall not accept the [first] four from you after this except through it."'"

Furthermore, in what was reported from ‘Umar regarding the revelation of the verse on the Day of ‘Arafah, there is another problem: all of them mention that some of the People of the Book [spoke to him]. In some of them, it was Ka‘b who said to ‘Umar: "There is a verse in the Qur’an—if it had been revealed to us, the assembly of Jews, we would have taken the day it was revealed as a festival (عيد)—and it is His saying: **الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ** (Today I have perfected your religion for you)." ‘Umar said to him: "By Allah, I certainly know the day—it was the Day of ‘Arafah during the Farewell Pilgrimage."

The wording of what ibn Rahawayh and ‘Abd ibn Humayd narrated from Abu al-‘Aliyah is as follows: He said: "They were with ‘Umar and mentioned this verse. A man from the People of the Book said: 'If we knew which day this verse was revealed, we would have taken it as a festival.' ‘Umar said: 'Praise be to Allah who made it a festival for us—and the second day; it was revealed on the Day of ‘Arafah—and the second day is the Day of Sacrifice (يوم النحر), so He perfected the matter for us. Thus we knew that the matter after that is in decline (انتقاص).'"

What the end of the narration contains is reported in another form. In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, from ibn Abi Shaybah and ibn Jarir from ‘Antarah, he said: "When **الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ** (Today I have perfected your religion for you) was revealed—and that was on the day of the Great Pilgrimage—‘Umar wept. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to him: 'What makes you weep?' He said: 'It made me weep that we were in an increase in our religion; but since it has become perfect, nothing has ever become perfect but that it then declines.' He said: 'You have spoken the truth.'"

Similar to this narration in one respect is another narration also reported in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ahmad from ‘Alqamah ibn ‘Abdillah al-Mazani, who said: "A man narrated to me: 'I was in the assembly of ‘Umar ibn al-Khattab. ‘Umar said to a man from the people: "How did you hear the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) describing Islam?" He said: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: Indeed, Islam began as a young camel (جذعا), then a two-year-old (ثنيا), then a four-year-old (رباعيا), then a six-year-old (سدسيا), then a full-grown one (بازلا)." ‘Umar said: "And what is after full growth but decline?"'"

These narrations—as you can see—aim to explain that the meaning of the verse’s revelation on the Day of ‘Arafah was to draw the people’s attention to what they were witnessing of the manifestation of the religion’s cause and its independence in Mecca during the season. They interpret the perfection of the religion and the completion of the blessing as the clarity of the atmosphere in Mecca and the purity of the matter for the Muslims on that day, such that no religion was worshiped there then except their religion, without them fearing their enemies or being wary of them.

In other words, what is meant by the perfection of the religion and the completion of the blessing is the perfection of what was in their hands to act upon, without their enemies mixing with them or them being tasked with being wary of them—rather than the religion in the sense of the Shari‘ah (الشريعة) established by Allah consisting of doctrines and laws. Likewise, what is meant by Islam is the outward Islam present in their hands in the sphere of action.

If you wish, you could say: What is meant by the religion is the observed form of the religion from their actions, and likewise for Islam; for it is this meaning that accepts decline after increase.

As for the universal doctrines and laws legislated by Allah, they do not accept decline after the increase referred to by his saying in the narration: "Nothing has ever become perfect but that it then declines." For that is a cosmic law (سنة كونية) that also runs through history and society following the cosmos. As for the religion, it is not governed by such laws and norms except according to one who says: "Religion is a social norm that evolves and changes like other social norms."

Once you know this, you will realize that it is refuted, firstly: by the fact that what was mentioned as the meaning of the perfection of the religion does not truly apply to His saying, the Exalted: **الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ** (Today I have perfected your religion for you), as has been previously explained.

Secondly: How can Allah, the Glorified, count the religion in the form it appeared as perfect and attribute it to Himself as a favor (امتنانا) merely because the land was free of the outward presence of polytheists, and the society was outwardly upon Islam, free from their polytheist enemies—while among them were those whose harm and corruption were more severe than the polytheists, namely the hypocrites (المنافقون), given their secret societies, their infiltration into the ranks of the Muslims, their corruption of the situation, their subversion of affairs, their meddling in the religion, and their casting of doubts? They had a significant presence, as addressed by numerous verses of the Qur’an, such as Surah al-Munafiqun and what is in Surahs al-Baqarah, al-Nisa’, al-Ma'idah, al-Anfal, al-Bara’ah, al-Ahzab, and others.

I wonder, where did their assembly go? How were their breaths extinguished? By what path was their plot nullified and their falsehood vanished? How could it be correct, in their presence, for Allah to bestow a favor upon the Muslims on that day by perfecting the outward form of their religion, completing the outward blessing upon them, and approving the outward form of Islam, merely because their enemies among the polytheists were driven from Mecca—while the hypocrites were more hostile than them, greater in danger, and more bitter in impact! The confirmation of this is His saying, the Exalted, addressing His Prophet regarding them: **هُمُ الْعَدُوُّ فَاحْذَرْهُمْ** (They are the enemy, so beware of them) (al-Munafiqun: 4).

How could Allah, the Glorified, bestow a favor and describe as perfect the outward form of a religion whose inward was such, or mention His blessings as complete when they were mixed with affliction, or inform of His pleasure with a form of Islam that had this meaning! Allah has said: **وَما كُنْتُ مُتَّخِذَ الْمُضِلِّينَ عضداً** (nor do I take those who mislead as aides) (al-Kahf: 51), and He said regarding the hypocrites—and He intended nothing but their religion: **فَإِنْ تَرْضَوْا عَنْهُمْ فَإِنَّ اللهَ لا يَرْضى عَنِ الْقَوْمِ الْفاسِقِينَ** (for if you are pleased with them, Allah is indeed not pleased with the transgressing lot) (al-Bara’ah: 96). The verse, after all this, is absolute (مطلقة); it did not restrict any of the perfection, completion, or pleasure, nor the religion, Islam, or blessing, to one aspect over another.

If you say: The verse—as previously hinted—is a fulfillment of the promise contained in His saying, the Exalted: **وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَيَسْتَخْلِفَنَّهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ كَمَا اسْتَخْلَفَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ وَلَيُمَكِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ دِينَهُمُ الَّذِي ارْتَضى لَهُمْ وَلَيُبَدِّلَنَّهُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ خَوْفِهِمْ أَمْناً يَعْبُدُونَنِي لا يُشْرِكُونَ بِي شَيْئاً** (Allah has promised those of you who have faith and do righteous deeds that He will surely make them successors in the earth, even as He made those who were before them successors, and He will surely establish for them their religion which He has approved for them, and that He will surely give them security in exchange for their fear, [and] they will worship Me, not ascribing any partners to Me) (al-Nur: 55).

The verse, as you see, promises them the establishment of their religion which He has approved for them. This corresponds in this verse to His saying: **أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ** (I have perfected your religion for you) and His saying: **وَرَضِيتُ لَكُمُ الْإِسْلامَ دِيناً** (and I have approved Islam as your religion). Thus, what is meant by the perfection of their approved religion is its establishment for them—meaning its purification from the interference of the polytheists. As for the hypocrites, their matter is something other than interference. This is the meaning indicated by the narrations of its revelation on the Day of ‘Arafah, and the group mentions that what is meant by it is the purification of religious acts and those Muslims performing them from the interference of the polytheists and their pilgrimage.

I say: That the verse **الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ** (Today I have perfected) is among the instances of fulfilling what was promised in His saying: **وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** (Allah has promised those who have faith...), and likewise that His saying in this verse: **أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ** (I have perfected your religion for you) corresponds to His saying: **وَلَيُمَكِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ دِينَهُمُ الَّذِي ارْتَضى لَهُمْ** (and He will surely establish for them their religion which He has approved for them) in that verse and conveys its meaning—all of that is beyond doubt.

However, the verse of Surah al-Nur begins with His saying: **وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ** (Allah has promised those of you who have faith and do righteous deeds). These are a specific group of Muslims whose outward actions agree with their inward, and what is in the rank of their actions of the religion corresponds to and matches what is with Allah, the Glorified, of the legislated religion. Thus, the establishment of their religion, which is approved by Allah, the Glorified, for them, is the perfection of what is in the knowledge and will of Allah of the approved religion by casting it into the mold of legislation (التشريع) and gathering its parts for them through revelation (الإنزال), so that they may worship Him through it after the despair of those who disbelieve in their religion.

This is what we mentioned: that the meaning of His perfecting the religion is its perfection from the perspective of legislating the obligations (الفرائض); thus, no obligation was legislated after the revelation of the verse. It is not the purification of their actions—especially their pilgrimage—from the actions and pilgrimage of the polytheists, such that their actions do not mix with theirs. In other words, the meaning of perfecting the religion is raising it to the highest stages of progress so that it does not accept decline after increase.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, he said: My father narrated to me from Safwan ibn Yahya from al-‘Ala’ from Muhammad ibn Muslim from Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام), who said: "The last obligation (فريضة) He revealed was mastership (الولاية)—then He did not reveal any obligation after it. Then He revealed: **الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ** (Today I have perfected your religion for you) at Kura‘ al-Ghamim. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) established it at al-Juhfah—and He did not reveal any obligation after it."

I say: al-Tabarsi narrated this meaning in *al-Majma‘* from the two Imams: al-Baqir and al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), and al-Ayyashi narrated it in his *Tafsir* from Zurarah from al-Baqir (عليه السلام).

In the *Amali* of the Shaykh, with its chain of transmission to Muhammad ibn Ja‘far ibn Muhammad from his father Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) from ‘Ali, the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام), who said: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: 'The building of Islam is upon five traits: upon the two testimonies (الشهادتين) and the two companions (القرينتين).'" It was said to him: "As for the two testimonies, we know them; but what are the two companions?"

He said: "Prayer and zakat—for one of them is not accepted except with the other; and fasting and the pilgrimage to the House of Allah for whoever is able to find a way to it. And He sealed that with mastership (الولاية), so Allah (عز وجل) revealed: **الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَأَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِي وَرَضِيتُ لَكُمُ الْإِسْلامَ دِيناً** (Today I have perfected your religion for you, and I have completed My blessing upon you, and I have approved Islam as your religion)."

In *Rawdat al-Wa‘izin* by al-Fattal ibn al-Farisi from Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام), he mentioned the story of the Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) departure for the pilgrimage, then his appointing ‘Ali for mastership upon his return to Medina and the revelation of the verse. It contains the sermon of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) on the Day of Ghadir, which is a very long sermon.

I say: al-Tabarsi narrated the like of it in *al-Ihtijaj* with a connected chain of transmission from al-Hadrami from Abu Ja‘far al-Baqir (عليه السلام). The revelation of the verse regarding mastership was also narrated by al-Kulayni in *al-Kafi* and al-Saduq in *al-‘Uyun*, both with chains of transmission from ‘Abd al-‘Aziz ibn Muslim from al-Rida (عليه السلام). The Shaykh also narrated its revelation regarding it in his *Amali* with his chain to ibn Abi ‘Umayr from al-Mufaddal ibn ‘Umar from al-Sadiq from his grandfather the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام). al-Tabarsi also narrated it in *al-Majma‘* with his chain to Abu Harun al-‘Abdi from Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri. The Shaykh also narrated it in his *Amali* with his chain to Ishaq ibn Isma‘il al-Naysaburi from al-Sadiq from his fathers from al-Hasan ibn ‘Ali (عليه السلام). We have omitted the narrations due to their length, preferring brevity; whoever desires them should refer to their sources, and Allah is the Guide.
